summary: Drew DeVault announces the public alpha of sr.ht, a 100% free and open source software forge designed as a fundamentally different alternative to GitHub and GitLab. Unlike competitors that clone GitHub's model, sr.ht embraces email-based development workflows favored by major projects like Linux and PostgreSQL. The platform is extremely lightweight (under 10 KiB per page, no JavaScript, no tracking) and highly modular, with each component working both within sr.ht and with external tools. The flagship CI platform, builds.sr.ht, is positioned as the most capable available, supporting everything from Linux distributions to BSD and Hurd. DeVault emphasizes that sr.ht is fully copyleft-licensed with no proprietary premium tier, unlike GitLab's open-core model.
key_points: sr.ht is 100% open source under copyleft, distinguishing it from GitHub (mostly closed) and GitLab (open core with proprietary premium)
The platform embraces email-based patch workflows used by Linux, PostgreSQL, and git itself, rather than cloning GitHub's pull request model
Pages average under 10 KiB with no JavaScript and no tracking, making it one of the most lightweight web platforms available
builds.sr.ht is powerful enough to serve entire Linux distributions and even non-Linux operating systems like BSD and Hurd
The modular architecture allows each component to be used independently or integrated with external tools like GitHub
money_quotes: Where many forges attempt to replicate GitHub's success with a thinly veiled clone of the GitHub UI and workflow, sr.ht is fundamentally different in its approach.

Unlike GitHub, which is almost entirely closed source, and Gitlab, which is mostly open source but with a proprietary premium offering, all of sr.ht is completely open source, with a copyleft license.
sr.ht is one of the most lightweight websites on the internet, with the average page weighing less than 10 KiB, with no tracking and no JavaScript.
Unlike GitHub, which favors its own in-house pull request workflow, sr.ht embraces and improves upon the email-based workflow favored by git itself.

It's so powerful that I've been working with multiple Linux distributions on bringing them onboard because it's the only platform which can scale to the automation needs of an entire Linux distribution.
It's so powerful that I've been working with maintainers of non-Linux operating systems, from BSD to even Hurd, because it's the only platform which can even consider supporting their needs.
key_insight: A truly free software forge must not just be open source but fundamentally rethink the development workflow, embracing the email-based, modular approach that powers the world's most important open source projects rather than cloning GitHub's proprietary model.
